1:
The chapter opens on Joab's initiative, and the witnesses divide first over a single Hebrew preposition. The grammatical-historical commentators press its ambiguity. Ellicott points out that the clause, like the last verse of the previous chapter, may be read in opposite senses: either David's heart yearned for Absalom (so the Authorised Version, the Vulgate, the Septuagint, and the Syriac) or it was set against him. The preposition bears both meanings, and in the only other place the exact phrase occurs (Daniel 11:28) it unquestionably expresses hostility. On the hostile reading Joab's whole stratagem becomes necessary, since a father merely longing after his son would need no elaborate ruse. Keil and Delitzsch keep the traditional sense but supply the motive: David had not repealed the banishment even after he was comforted for Amnon's death, so Joab set himself to bring Absalom back by craft, sending to Tekoah for a woman shrewd enough to change the king's mind.

The puritan Matthew Henry gives the fullest anatomy of Joab's designs, numbering four. As a courtier he studied to ingratiate himself by gratifying the king's secret wish; as a friend he had a kindness for Absalom, whom he looked on as the rising sun; as a statesman he feared that Absalom's continued exile might, should David die, kindle a civil war, for the people's darling was much beloved; and as a delinquent himself, guilty of Abner's blood, he knew that any favour procured for a manslayer would help to secure his own reprieve. Keil concurs in this last, colder calculation: Absalom had the best prospect of the throne, and Joab thought this the surest way to shield himself from punishment for the murder he had done — though, as Keil notes, the issue frustrated every such hope.

The reformed Gill simply fixes Joab as the general of David's army, the man best placed to read and to work upon his master's mood. The wesleyan Wesley resolves the tension of the preposition into the king's own inner conflict: David desired to see him, but was ashamed to show kindness to one whom God's law and his own conscience obliged him to punish; he wanted a fair pretence, and Joab supplied it. So where the schools seem to differ over whether the king's heart leaned toward Absalom or against him, Wesley's reading quietly holds both — a suppressed longing checked by shame — while all agree that Joab, whatever his motives, perceived the opening and moved at once to exploit it.
2:
The witnesses turn to the stagecraft of Joab's plot, and they agree that every detail was chosen to disarm the king. The grammatical-historical commentators settle the geography and the woman's character. Ellicott places Tekoah on its high hill some five miles south of Bethlehem, the home of the prophet Amos and the native town of Ira, one of David's thirty heroes (2 Samuel 23:26); it lay near enough to Joab's Bethlehem for him to have known this 'wise woman' personally, and Ellicott is careful to add that there is no ground whatever for suspecting her of witchcraft or disrepute. Gill puts her in Judah, twelve miles from Jerusalem, and repeats the tradition that she may have been an ancestress of Amos; he grasps the tactical reason for the distance — the scene was laid far off so that the case could not readily be inquired into. Keil and Delitzsch linger over the text, defending the reading that the woman spake to the king against the many manuscripts that have her come to him.

The wesleyan Clarke draws out the artistry most fully, listing the circumstances that combined to make one united impression on the king's heart: she was a widow, and so the more pitiable; she lived at a distance, which made detection harder; she was advanced in years, as Josephus says, that her suit might carry more weight; she put on mourning to heighten the sense of distress; she framed a case resembling David's own; and she did not make the parallel too plain, lest the king should see her intention before granting the pardon. Wesley adds the small telling touch that she was told not to anoint herself, as mourners refrained from the oil they used when out of grief.

The schools do not contend here; they marvel together at a scheme in which nothing is accidental. Ellicott's observation is the governing one: the parable that follows was contrived by Joab, yet its execution required real skill and address in the woman, and it was purposely made not too closely parallel to Absalom's case, so that it should not defeat its own object before the king had committed himself. The whole verse is a study in how a true instinct for mercy in the king could be turned, by a practised hand, into a trap for his own judgement.
3:
This brief verse — Joab's instruction that the woman come to the king and speak the words he had put in her mouth — draws only a short notice from the reformed Gill, who marks simply that she was to appear before the king in the disconsolate condition already described. Yet the witnesses' silence is itself eloquent, and the verse repays attention to what it plainly says. The decisive clause is the last: Joab put the words in her mouth. The scheme is wholly his; the woman is his instrument, and the speech she will deliver is a script prepared in advance. Keil and Delitzsch, treating these opening verses together, stress just this — that Joab framed both the disguise and the discourse, so that when the king is moved it is Joab's craft, not the woman's grief, that has moved him.

The dramaturgy is worth weighing. A widow's private petition would have easy access to a king who, as the psalmist sang, is himself to be a father of the fatherless and a judge of the widows (Ps. 68:5); David's known tenderness toward mourners is the very hinge on which the plot turns. By rehearsing her part beforehand, Joab ensures that the case will unfold in stages, each drawing a further concession, until the king has bound himself past retreat. The woman must therefore be more than a messenger: she must sustain a character, improvise under questioning, and reserve the application until the judgement is already given.

There is no debate among the witnesses at this point, only a quiet consensus that the verse exposes the machinery of the whole chapter. What looks, in the verses to come, like a spontaneous cry for help is in truth a carefully mounted appeal, and the reader is told so in advance. The honesty of the narrator in showing us the strings — so Joab put the words in her mouth — is of a piece with Scripture's habit of never flattering its heroes: David's mercy will be shown to be real, but so will the manipulation that exploits it, and the same king who is easily led here will pay dearly for the half-measures that follow.
4:
The woman now enters the royal presence, falls on her face to the ground, does obeisance, and cries Help, O king! The witnesses here are chiefly the grammatical-historical commentators, and their interest is textual and dramatic rather than doctrinal. Keil and Delitzsch and Ellicott both note the well-attested variant: many manuscripts, together with the Septuagint, the Vulgate, and the Syriac, read that the woman came to the king, where the Masoretic text has that she spake to him. Ellicott judges the difference immaterial, and Keil, weighing whether came could be an accidental corruption, decides it could not — the received reading stands. Gill fills the scene with the courtesy of approach, supposing that Joab had introduced her and that she opened, as suppliants did, with a salutation of long life to the king.

The gesture itself carries the weight of the moment. To fall prostrate and cry for help is the posture of one whose only recourse is the throne — the very picture of helplessness that Joab had designed. The single word Help is a legal appeal as much as an emotional one: it invokes the king in his office as the last court of the desperate, the judge to whom the powerless come when every lower remedy has failed. The woman thus presents herself, from the first instant, as a case already lost at law and thrown wholly on the royal mercy.

There is no disagreement among the witnesses, and the verse is a hinge: the disguise is complete, the approach is made, and the king's compassion is engaged before a single fact of the case has been stated. What follows will be a graduated conversation in which the woman, having secured a hearing by her very prostration, draws the king step by step from a general promise to a sworn and irreversible pledge. The narrator's textual precision — whether she came or spake — only underlines how carefully the scene has been staged, and how much turns on the exact words that pass between the widow of Tekoah and the king of Israel. Even the obeisance is calculated: it flatters the king's justice while binding him, by the courtesies of his own court, to hear the suit of one who has thrown herself, quite literally, at his feet.
5:
The king's gentle question, What aileth thee?, opens the woman's feigned tale, and the witnesses read the verse both for its craft and for the theology of mercy woven into it. Gill hears in the question David's readiness to grant help once the case is known. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch lays out the whole fabricated calamity the woman is about to unfold: she is a widow, her two sons quarrelled in the field with none to part them, one killed the other, and now the whole family demands that the survivor be given up to the avenger of blood — which would destroy the heir, quench her last surviving coal, and leave her husband neither name nor remnant upon the earth. Keil observes the grammar of ordinary speech in the doubled object of he smote him, the other, defending it against proposed emendation.

The wesleyan voices attend to the shrewdness of the chosen character. Clarke thinks it very possible that the principal facts were real, and that Joab had found a person whose true circumstances nearly matched the story he needed — a touch that would make the tale ring the more convincingly. Wesley draws out why a widow was the perfect mask: she is one of those who most need compassion, and whom the king was by God's own law bound in a singular manner to relieve. The disguise is not merely pathetic but legally pointed, engaging the king precisely where his duty and his tenderness meet.

The witnesses do not divide; they trace together the opening move of a masterly appeal. Behind the fiction stands a real and terrible pressure — the ancient law of blood-vengeance, under which a bereaved family might exact the surviving brother's life. The woman frames her case so that mercy to the manslayer appears not as the defeat of justice but as the rescue of a widow's whole future, the preservation of a name from extinction. It is this framing — a hopeless suppliant, a sympathetic wrong, a remedy only the king can give — that will presently allow her to turn the king's own compassionate judgement back upon himself and his banished son.
6:
The woman's account of her two sons — how they strove together in the field, with none to part them, and one smote and slew the other — is read by the witnesses for the careful way it shapes the king's sympathy. Gill notes the deliberate detail of two sons, the number required so that the invented case might answer to Amnon and Absalom, the two sons of David whose story lies behind it. Keil and Delitzsch treats the verse as the point at which the plan takes effect, the plausible domestic tragedy that secures the king's favourable hearing.

The grammatical-historical Ellicott makes the sharpest observation, and it is the one that governs the whole parable. The woman represents the fratricide as unpremeditated and without malice — a sudden, unwitnessed quarrel in the open field, with no one to intervene. This, Ellicott insists, made her case essentially different from Absalom's, for Absalom had killed Amnon deliberately, nursing an old grudge, at a feast, before his guests. But that difference is precisely what the woman conceals for now: at this point of the story her object is only to dispose the king favourably toward the culprit, and by the time the application is reached the disparity will have passed out of the king's mind. The very feature that ought to have exposed the analogy is quietly suppressed so that the analogy may work.

The witnesses agree, then, that this verse is the hinge of the deception's logic. A killing in hot blood, without witnesses, is the most pardonable kind of homicide, the sort the law of the cities of refuge was designed to shelter; a killing in cold blood, planned and public, is murder proper. By painting her son's deed in the softest colours, the woman wins from David a judgement of mercy that fits her fictional case far better than it fits the real one she means to plead. The reader, forewarned that Joab has scripted the whole, watches the trap being carefully baited — a trap whose spring depends on the king forgetting, at the crucial moment, the very distinction Ellicott here draws so plainly.
7:
The woman's picture of the whole family risen against her — demanding the surviving son for the avenger of blood, that they may quench my coal which is left and leave her husband neither name nor remainder — draws out the witnesses' interest in her imagery and in the covert design beneath it. Gill marks that it is not merely the next of kin but the entire kindred who rise as one man, clamouring for justice. The wesleyan Clarke explains the metaphor with care: a man and his descendants are often called in Scripture a lamp or a light, as when David's men swore that he should no more go to battle lest he quench the light of Israel (2 Sam. 21:17), and as to raise up a lamp to a person signifies giving him posterity (Ps. 132:17); so to quench her coal is to extinguish her family from among the people. He notes that the heathen used the same figure, calling the survivors of Deucalion's flood living coals, by whom the vital flame of mankind was to be rekindled.

The grammatical-historical Ellicott exposes the hidden barb. The woman puts into the family's mouth the words we will destroy the heir also, because that would be the effect of what they proposed; and by this stroke, he says, the effect of the parable is greatly heightened, for there is beyond doubt a covert allusion to Absalom as the heir of David. What sounds like a widow's private terror is quietly loaded with the political stake the whole nation feels. Keil and Delitzsch, looking ahead to the king's reply in the next verse, judges his promise of protection perfectly just: if the brothers had merely quarrelled and one had killed the other in heat, the survivor rightly deserved shelter from the avenger.

The witnesses do not dispute; they admire the double edge of the woman's rhetoric. On its surface the speech is a mother's plea to save her one remaining child and her husband's name from oblivion; underneath, it presses upon the king the intolerable thought of a royal line cut short and an heir destroyed. The pathos and the politics are fused in a single image of a dying coal, and the king, moved by the one, is being led without knowing it toward the other.
8:
The king's first response — Go to thine house, and I will give charge concerning thee — is read by the witnesses as a genuine but insufficient concession, and their brief comments fasten on why the woman cannot rest in it. Gill takes the words at face value as an invitation to go home and be easy, the king undertaking to see her righted. But the wesleyan Clarke catches the reason she presses on: this promise was too distant; a general charge to look into the matter could not bring her business to the conclusion she needed, and so she proceeds. Keil and Delitzsch, weighing the same reply, defends the justice of it — the king promises that the necessary orders will be given so that the avenger of blood shall not slay her son — and finds the pledge entirely proper on the facts as they were laid before him.

The interest of the verse lies in this gap between a real mercy and an adequate one. David does not dismiss the suppliant with a cold let the law take its course; his instinct is toward compassion, and he opens the door. Yet a promise to give charge is only an undertaking to consider and to act in due season, and the woman, schooled by Joab, knows that half a grant is no grant. Her whole art will now be to convert this vague benevolence, step by step, into a definite, sworn, and irreversible security for her son's life, refusing at each stage to be satisfied with anything less than the next concession.

The witnesses do not divide, but the verse quietly foreshadows the chapter's deeper theme. David is a man whose kindness outruns his firmness, ready to promise more than justice strictly allows and yet unable, at each stage, to close the matter cleanly. The very readiness that makes him grant the widow's first request is the readiness that Joab has counted on, and the graduated pressure she is about to apply mirrors the graduated weakness by which David will, in the end, restore an unrepentant Absalom to his own house and then to his own presence.
9:
The woman's next move — the iniquity be on me, and on my father's house; and the king and his throne be guiltless — is read by the witnesses as a calculated deepening of the appeal. Gill hears her speaking with much vehemence yet great respect, dreading any inquisition into a case that, being a fable, could not bear scrutiny, and therefore anxious to have sentence pronounced at once. The grammatical-historical commentators expose the tactic. Ellicott observes that although the king has already granted her request, the woman seeks to prolong the interview so as to lead him to commit himself more completely; Keil and Delitzsch calls her crafty and notes that she is working to bind the king by an oath, that she may hold him the more firmly.

The wesleyan voices probe the moral logic of her offer to bear the blame. Clarke reads it as her intimation that, should the king fear his clemency might reflect on the administration of justice in the land, she is willing that all reproach should attach to her and her family, leaving the king and his throne clear. Wesley draws out the veiled pressure with precision: if through the king's forgetfulness or neglect her adversaries should prevail and destroy her son, let God not lay it to the king's charge but to herself — a form of words that insinuates such an omission would in fact bring guilt upon him, and yet is so decently ordered as not to seem to blame or threaten the king. Wesley thinks this best explains David's answer in the next verse, which shows that she had asked, in effect, for firmer assurance.

The witnesses agree in admiring the woman's skill without approving her cause. She has learned that a single concession from a king is worth little unless it is sealed, and she manufactures a scruple — the honour of the throne — precisely in order to have it waved aside, so that David, in clearing himself of any charge of injustice, is drawn to make his mercy the more absolute. It is the art of the suppliant who, by seeming to protect the judge, tightens her hold upon his word.
10:
The king's fuller pledge — whosoever saith aught unto thee, bring him to me, and he shall not touch thee any more — is met by the witnesses as a second advance that still falls short of the woman's goal. Gill notes that the words meet the case of any who would reproach her or demand her son's surrender: the king now offers her his personal protection against all molestation. Yet the wesleyan Clarke marks precisely why even this does not satisfy her: it did not bring the matter to such a bearing that she could reach her conclusion, which was to get the king pledged by a solemn promise that all proceedings against her son should be stopped altogether. A guarantee against harassment is not yet a guarantee of the son's life.

The verse shows the king moving, stage by stage, exactly as the woman intends. First he promised to give charge; now he undertakes to silence and summon any who trouble her; but he has not yet sworn that the avenger of blood shall be stayed. Each concession is larger than the last, and each is drawn from him by her refusal to be content — a refusal that wears the guise of a poor woman's timid persistence but is in truth a practised advocate's method. Magistrates, as commentators on the passage observe, ought indeed to be the patrons of oppressed widows; David's growing warmth is the right instinct of a just king, and it is that very rightness which is being turned against him.

There is no debate among the witnesses. They trace a single ascending line: the woman will not stop until protection has become pardon and pardon has been sealed by an oath. The reader, knowing the case is fictitious and the end political, watches the king's real virtue — his care for the defenceless — become the instrument of his deception. What is admirable in David as a judge is precisely what makes him vulnerable as a father, and the woman of Tekoah, or rather Joab behind her, has measured that vulnerability exactly.
11:
Here the woman reaches the pledge she has been angling for: let the king remember the LORD thy God, that the avenger of blood destroy no more, lest they destroy her son — and the king swears, As the LORD liveth, there shall not one hair of thy son fall to the earth. The witnesses read the verse as the climax of her craft and the point of no return. Gill takes her plea two ways: as a call for the king to imitate the gracious and merciful God, pitying the distressed, and perhaps also as a demand for an oath — remember the LORD in the sense of swearing by him. The grammatical-historical Ellicott is blunt: having succeeded so far, the crafty woman now leads the king to bind himself with the solemnity of an oath.

The wesleyan voices bring out the urgency and the weight of the appeal. Clarke reads her as pressing that God, when earnestly entreated for mercy, shows it promptly and does not wait till the case is hopeless; the danger to her son is imminent, and unless the king decides instantly it may be too late. The king's answering As the LORD liveth is, he says, the most solemn promise and oath — exactly what the woman set out to extort. Wesley gives her plea a sharper turn: remember the Lord means remember him in whose presence this promise is made, and who will be a witness against the king if he break it.

The puritan Matthew Henry lifts the king's oath to its highest sense. The pledge that not one hair shall fall to the earth is an undertaking that the son shall come to no harm; and Henry hears in it a shadow of a greater assurance, for the Son of David has promised all who put themselves under his protection that, though they should be put to death for his sake, not a hair of their head shall perish (Luke 21:16–18). He recalls too the solemnity of an oath by which God, willing to show the immutability of his counsel, confirms his promise (Heb. 6:17–18). So the witnesses agree: with this sworn word the king has passed the point from which he cannot withdraw, and the woman has only to apply her fable to the son the king has never named.
12:
Having secured her oath, the woman asks leave to speak one word more, and the witnesses see the mask beginning to slip. Gill marks the turn: her point now gained and confirmed by oath, she proceeds to accommodate the parable and apply it to Absalom, improving her fictitious case in his favour. The king, pleased with her wit, grants the further word, not yet seeing where it tends.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch does the close work on her cautious, deliberately veiled language. Her question — wherefore then hast thou thought such a thing against the people of God? — is, he shows, intentionally indefinite, hinting at rather than stating her charge; and the clause that follows, that in speaking as he has the king is as one who inculpates himself since he will not fetch home his own banished one, is left half-said on purpose. Keil rejects the reading that would make her words concern the protection extended to herself; they look forward, obliquely, to Absalom. The whole verse is a threshold: the suppliant is about to become a reprover, the private petition a public remonstrance.

The witnesses do not divide, and the verse rewards attention to its dramatic structure. Everything the woman has done so far — the disguise, the tale, the graduated concessions, the sworn oath — has been machinery to reach this single pivot, where the case she pleaded for her imaginary son can be turned upon the king's real one. She moves warily, asking permission, softening her approach, because she is about to say what no subject could safely say unbidden: that the king stands condemned by his own judgement. As Nathan had once brought David to sentence himself by a parable of a ewe lamb, so now, by a parable of two quarrelling sons, the woman of Tekoah prepares to make the king the judge and the defendant in one. The reader feels the hinge turn, and waits for the application that the next verses will drive home. That she asks leave at all, rather than blurting out her charge, measures the danger of what she is about to do: to tell a king he has erred is a peril no flattery can wholly disarm, and she ventures it only because his own sworn judgement has already armed her with his consent.
13:
Now the woman turns her fable upon the king: wherefore then hast thou thought such a thing against the people of God? for the king doth speak this thing as one which is faulty, in that the king doth not fetch home again his banished. The witnesses gather around the meaning of the people of God and the force of the self-condemnation. The grammatical-historical Ellicott insists the phrase can, by constant usage, mean only Israel: the king is in some way wronging the nation, and doing so against the very spirit of the judgement he has just given, by leaving Absalom — whom she pointedly does not name — in banishment. Her language is cautious, hinting rather than declaring, and Ellicott renders the crux more literally: from the king's speaking this word he is as one guilty. Gill records an older view that the people of God might even denote Absalom and his men, or Absalom alone, but takes the plain sense to be the nation's grief at the king's severity.

The wesleyan voices sharpen the argument from consistency. Clarke frames the woman's conclusion crisply: the king is willing to pardon a stranger's fratricide at a poor widow's plea, yet unwilling to pardon his own son, whose restoration the whole nation desires — is that clemency to be refused to the heir of the throne which is granted to a private man? Wesley presses the same edge: if the king would not suffer the avengers to destroy two obscure persons, how unreasonable to pursue Amnon's blood upon Absalom, whose death would grieve the whole commonwealth; by his own word and oath, given for her son, the king condemnest thyself for denying the like equity to his own.

The witnesses candidly own that the analogy is imperfect. Absalom did not kill in a sudden passion but in cold, long-nursed malice, not in a lonely field but at a feast before his guests; he was no only son, for David had others, including the child God loved. Yet, as Henry and Wesley both note, David was too well affected to his son to be critical of the disparity, and more eager than the woman herself to apply to his own case the merciful judgement he had given for hers. Change but the name, Henry writes with the Latin poet, and the tale is told of thee. The parable has done its work; the king's own mercy is now the argument against his own severity.
14:
The woman's great sentence — we must needs die, and are as water spilt on the ground, which cannot be gathered up again; neither doth God respect any person: yet doth he devise means, that his banished be not expelled from him — is the theological summit of the chapter, and the witnesses treat it with corresponding weight. Gill exposes its rhetorical drift: all must die; she insinuates that David too must die and a disputed succession may follow, that Amnon would have died in time regardless, and that Absalom must die eventually — so why hasten it? Gill judges this, as an argument for sparing a real murderer, to be poor reasoning, however plausible.

The grammatical-historical commentators wrestle with the difficult clause about the soul. Clarke calls the whole verse elegant and powerfully persuasive, but records the variants: the Hebrew reads literally God doth not take away the soul, the Septuagint God will receive the soul — which he takes to attest the ancient Jewish belief in the soul's separate existence after death — and the Vulgate nor does God will the destruction of the soul. Ellicott gives the sense that best fits the argument: God doth not take away the soul, but thinketh thoughts that he may not banish the banished one; that is, God in his wrath remembers mercy and does not press punishment to the extreme. He warns that the woman craftily withdraws the king's attention from the real question of right and justice to the safer ground of life's uncertainty. Wesley expounds the human reference plainly — thou, O king, Absalom, and Amnon must all die — and finds in the closing clause the provision of the law by which the manslayer, banished to a city of refuge, might return at the high priest's death.

The puritan Matthew Henry gathers the verse into a gospel. He finds in it two great instances of divine mercy urged as motives for human mercy: the patience by which God bears with sinners and does not strike them dead in the act, and the provision by which, though sin has banished them, they need not be expelled for ever. The state of sinners is a state of banishment from God, and it is against his mind that any should so perish, for he is not willing that any should be lost but has devised means — atonement and sacrifice — to bring his banished home. This mercy of God, Henry adds with the Lord's own parable of the unforgiving servant (Matt. 18:32–33), should incline us to be merciful one to another. So beneath a woman's crafty plea the witnesses hear a true doctrine: that God devises means for the banished, though here it is pressed in the service of a very human and imperfect design.
15:
The woman returns to her own pretended affairs — now therefore that I am come to speak of this thing unto my lord the king, it is because the people have made me afraid — and the witnesses read the move as a deliberate return to disguise. Gill notes simply that she resumes the cover of her own case, spoken under a feigned character. The grammatical-historical Ellicott catches the studied ambiguity: she excuses her boldness in addressing the king by the pressure brought upon her from without, but whether the people means the nation at large (as the Absalom application requires) or merely her own family connections (as the fiction requires) is left intentionally and craftily doubtful. In the next breath she will keep up the pretence of reality by speaking again of her son, yet here she trades on a double meaning that lets both readings stand at once.

The wesleyan Wesley presses the political sense that lies beneath the veil. She was, he says, in effect forced to this bold address by the disposition of the king's own people, who were discontented at Absalom's perpetual banishment; the fear is that, should Absalom by his father-in-law's help invade the land, a populace that thought him hardly used might take up arms. On this reading the woman's confessed fright is a genuine warning smuggled inside a fiction — the nation's unrest, not a widow's, is the danger she truly reports.

The witnesses agree that the artistry lies in the ambiguity itself. Having brought the king to the very edge of the application, the woman half-retreats into her assumed part, so that if the king should resent the boldness she can plead the terrors of a poor woman, and if he should receive it she has planted the thought of a restless people. It is the manner of one who, having risked a hard truth, at once softens it with a plausible excuse. The verse thus keeps the two cases — the imaginary son and the banished prince — deliberately entangled, so that neither can be firmly seized apart from the other, and the king is left to draw the conclusion the woman dares not fully speak.
16:
The woman professes her confidence that the king will hear, to deliver his handmaid out of the hand of the man that would destroy me and my son together out of the inheritance of God, and the witnesses attend to her flattery and to the loaded phrase the inheritance of God. Gill takes her at her word as fully persuaded of the king's readiness, having already found him willing to hear. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch fixes the key term: the inheritance of God is the nation of Israel, God's own portion, as elsewhere in Scripture (1 Sam. 26:19; Deut. 32:9); to be cut off from it is to be driven out from the people of the LORD and the place of his worship.

The wesleyan Wesley draws out how the phrase carries the whole hidden argument. Spoken of her own son, it means being destroyed from the land of Israel; spoken of Absalom, it presses on the king the peril of a prince living in exile among idolaters, cut off from the inheritance of God, deprived of the law and ordinances that might bring him to repentance, and in danger of catching and carrying home the infection of heathen worship. Wesley also hears in me and my son together a mother whose life is bound up in her son's — a stroke the more affecting because it might be David's own case, and so touches him in a tender place — and understands the whole to suggest that the safety and comfort of the people of Israel hang upon Absalom's restoration.

The witnesses do not divide; they see one theme running through her words. The woman has moved the argument from bare justice to the welfare of the covenant nation and the spiritual danger of the exile, clothing a political appeal in the language of piety. That her son and Absalom should not be destroyed out of the inheritance of God sounds like a plea for a soul's preservation, and in Absalom's case it is made to sound as if his very godliness were at stake abroad — a generous construction his later conduct will bitterly disappoint. The verse shows how skilfully a true concern, the integrity of God's people, can be enlisted to serve a design that will in fact endanger it.
17:
The woman crowns her speech with high compliment: the word of my lord the king shall now be comfortable; for as an angel of God, so is my lord the king to discern good and bad; therefore the LORD thy God will be with thee. The witnesses weigh the flattery and its scriptural resonance. Gill renders the first clause for rest — a word to give ease and satisfaction not to her only but to all Israel, when they hear the king's resolve to bring Absalom home. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch expounds the angel comparison richly: the king is like the angel of God, the mediator of the covenant's blessings, in listening to every just complaint of his subjects and granting help to the oppressed; and Ellicott sets the phrase beside its parallels in the same books (2 Sam. 19:27; 1 Sam. 29:9), a recognised idiom for more-than-human discernment.

The wesleyan Wesley unfolds the logic of her closing benediction. The king is as an angel of God in wisdom, in justice, and in goodness; and therefore — because he is so gracious to those who in strict justice deserve punishment — God will own and stand by him in this act of grace, and prosper his undertakings. The compliment is not idle: it fixes upon the king a character he must now live up to, so that great expectations become, as Henry notes elsewhere, great engagements. Keil adds the shrewd observation that words so well considered and so crafty could not fail to show the king both what she really meant and that she had not come of her own accord — the very polish of her speech betrays the hand of Joab.

The witnesses agree that the verse is the seal of the woman's rhetoric and the trigger of the king's suspicion. She has flattered David into the likeness of an angel who discerns good and evil, only to have proved to him, by her own case, that he had failed to discern the good in his son's cause; and she has wrapped a subtle reproof in the softest praise. Her art has been perfect, and it is that very perfection — a poor widow speaking like a trained advocate — that will make David turn, in the next verse, to ask whose hand is really behind so finished a performance.
18:
The king, no longer deceived, says Hide not from me, I pray thee, the thing that I shall ask thee, and the woman answers, Let my lord the king now speak. The witnesses read the verse as the turning of the tables: the interrogator becomes interrogated. Gill observes that David now plainly understood the whole feigned case, and its intent — to let him know the mind of the people concerning Absalom's recall. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch draws the point sharply: the woman's words had been so well considered and so crafty that the king could not fail to perceive both what she really meant and that she had not come of her own accord; he therefore requires her to answer without disguise.

The exchange is brief but decisive. David's demand is courteous — I pray thee — yet it is the demand of a man who has caught the drift of a performance too polished to be spontaneous. The woman's reply is a graceful surrender: she does not resist the question but invites it, knowing that concealment is now impossible and that candour will serve her better than a lie. Her deference, let my lord the king speak, keeps the form of submission even as the fiction collapses.

The witnesses do not divide, and the verse marks a shift in the moral texture of the scene. Thus far the woman has controlled the dialogue, leading the king from concession to oath to self-condemnation; now the king reasserts himself, and the machinery of the plot must be owned. What is striking, as the next verse will show, is that the disclosure costs the woman nothing, for the king is not angry — he is charmed, and already half-resolved to do the very thing the plot was contrived to obtain. The unmasking, far from undoing Joab's design, will simply bring it into the open, so that David can grant from choice what he has been so cunningly led toward. There is even a mercy in the exposure, for it frees the king from being the dupe of a fiction: what he does henceforth he does knowingly, and the responsibility, for good or ill, is wholly his own. The woman's fable has served its single purpose, to open the king's mouth in his son's favour, and it may now be discarded.
19:
The king's blunt question — Is not the hand of Joab with thee in all this? — and the woman's frank confession draw the witnesses to the honesty that crowns her cunning. Keil and Delitzsch renders her reply closely: As thy soul liveth, my lord the king, none can turn to the right hand or to the left from aught that my lord the king hath spoken — the king always hits the very point — for thy servant Joab, he bade me, and he put all these words in the mouth of thine handmaid. Gill takes the king's question as an inference from the whole polish of the thing: who but the politic Joab, friend of Absalom, could have devised so shrewd a scheme?

The puritan Matthew Henry makes the woman's candour a moral example. The king soon suspected Joab, for he could not think such a woman would appeal to him unprompted in a matter of such weight; and the woman very honestly owned it, refusing to take to herself the praise of a well-managed scheme. If it be well done, let him have the thanks; if ill, let him bear the blame. Though she found her performance agreeable to the king, she spoke the truth as it was, and Henry turns it into a rule for every reader: never to tell a lie for the concealing of a plan, however cleverly managed. Dare to be true, he writes; nothing can need a lie.

The witnesses do not divide, and the verse holds a fine irony. The woman who has spent the whole interview in an elaborate fiction now, when challenged, will not add a single falsehood to defend it; her deceit was borrowed and official, undertaken at Joab's command, and she surrenders it the instant the king asks. Her flattery of David as one who discerns good and evil is here vindicated, for he has discerned the author of her errand at once. And her readiness to name Joab, far from spoiling the plot, completes it: the king now knows exactly what is asked and by whom, and is free, in the next verse, to grant it openly to the general who has so long desired it.
20:
The woman explains her whole errand — to fetch about this form of speech hath thy servant Joab done this thing — and closes with a last flattery, that the king is wise according to the wisdom of an angel of God, to know all things that are in the earth. Gill glosses her meaning: Joab had feigned a story so framed that it might be applied to Absalom's case and, to borrow the apostle's phrase, transferred in a figure to the king (1 Cor. 4:6). The design was disguise — to put the true suit into a shape the king would judge before he recognised it. Wesley agrees that Joab's aim was to propose his own and the people's desire for Absalom's restoration in this parabolical manner, and takes in the earth to mean in this land, the counsels and devices of all the king's subjects.

The wesleyan Clarke pauses on the extravagant compliment. To liken the king to an angel of God who knows all things upon earth is, he says, quite in the style of Asiatic flattery, where a great man is told that he can do everything and even that he is God. Clarke's note is a caution: the woman's praise, however graceful, is the hyperbole of the court, and its very excess should warn the reader against taking her estimate of David's discernment at face value — for the king, wise as he is, has just been led a long way by a fiction.

The witnesses do not divide, and the verse rounds off the episode of the wise woman. She has confessed the author, named the method, and flattered the judge, and in doing so has laid bare the whole rhetorical strategy of the chapter: a hard political request, unwelcome and unspeakable in plain terms, dressed as a private tragedy so that the king might pronounce upon it freely and then find the sentence turned against his own reluctance. It is the technique of Nathan's parable put to a very different use — not to convict a king of sin, but to coax him past his justice — and the witnesses let the contrast stand as its own quiet comment on the deed.
21:
The king yields: Behold now, I have done this thing: go therefore, bring the young man Absalom again. The witnesses divide over a small textual point and unite over a large theological one. The grammatical-historical commentators weigh the reading. Keil and Delitzsch defends the written text I have done as correct, treating the marginal thou hast done as a misunderstanding; Ellicott explains the difference in sense — I have done is simply a form of granting Joab's request, while thou hast done would carry an implied censure of Joab's stratagem, even as the king complies with his wish. Keil adds the telling inference that Joab had evidently pressed David for Absalom's return more than once before, which is why the king suspected his hand.

The puritan Matthew Henry turns the verse into a figure of the gospel, and this is the heart of the chapter's Christology. David, he notes, was himself inclined to favour Absalom, yet for the honour of his justice he would not do it but upon intercession made for him — and in this Henry sees the methods of divine grace. It is true that God has thoughts of compassion toward poor sinners, not willing that any should perish; yet he is reconciled to them only through a Mediator who intercedes on their behalf, and to whom he has given the charge, Go, bring them again. God was in Christ, reconciling the world unto himself (2 Cor. 5:19), and Christ came to this land of our banishment to bring us home to God.

The witnesses feel the tension between the type and its imperfection. As a picture of grace the scene is beautiful — a king who will not pardon without an intercessor, a mediator sent to fetch the banished home. Yet, as later verses insist, David's actual pardon of Absalom was a lapse of justice, not its honouring, and the analogy holds only at the level Henry draws it: that reconciliation comes through a mediator's pleading and the offended sovereign's willing consent. The very warmth that makes David a momentary emblem of the reconciling God is the weakness that will presently cost him his throne, and the witnesses let both truths stand — the gospel shadowed here, and the folly that darkens it.
22:
Joab falls on his face, blesses the king, and says, To day thy servant knoweth that I have found grace in thy sight, my lord, O king, in that the king hath fulfilled the request of his servant. The witnesses read his thanks against the grain of his history. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch finds it evident from these very words that Joab had frequently applied to David for Absalom's return without effect, which is why the king had suspected him behind the woman of Tekoah; the gratitude betrays how long the general had laboured for this end. Gill notes only the depth of Joab's obeisance, bowing to the ground in reverence.

The puritan Matthew Henry hears both courtesy and calculation. Joab returns thanks that the king should employ him in so universally welcome an errand, taking it as a personal kindness — and, some think, as a hint that David would never call him to account for the murder of Abner. But if Joab reasoned so, Henry warns, he was mistaken, as the event would show (1 Kings 2:5–6), where David's dying charge would at last reach the blood Joab had shed. The thanksgiving, so warm on its surface, rests on a false security.

The wesleyan Wesley strikes the note of judgement that runs beneath the scene. David, he says, had no power to dispense with God's laws, nor to spare any whom God had appointed to be punished, for the laws of God bound the kings and rulers of Israel as strictly as the people; and he asks how justly God would make this man, whom David so sinfully spared, a scourge to him. The witnesses thus set Joab's grateful bow in an ominous light: a favour granted against justice, received with thanks by a guilty man, and destined to breed the very troubles — Absalom's revolt and Joab's own reckoning — that the rest of the history will unfold. The verse is a moment of relief that the reader already knows to be the seed of sorrow, and the commentators mark it as such: a bow of thanks over a pardon that heaven had not sanctioned, and that earth would live to regret.
23:
The bare notice — So Joab arose and went to Geshur, and brought Absalom to Jerusalem — is handled briefly by the witnesses, who fill in its geography and its grave irregularity. Gill locates Geshur as the place where Absalom had taken refuge with his grandfather, its king (2 Sam. 13:37), whither he had fled after the murder of Amnon. Keil and Delitzsch records the errand simply: Joab went and fetched Absalom back to the capital. The action is swift and, on its face, a happy homecoming.

The puritan Matthew Henry, however, will not let the ease of it pass without protest. He cannot see how David is to be justified in suspending the execution of the ancient law that whoso sheddeth man's blood, by man shall his blood be shed (Gen. 9:6), a statute in which a righteous magistrate ought not to acknowledge even his own children. God's laws, Henry writes, were never designed to be like cobwebs, which catch the little flies but suffer the great ones to break through; and he sees a just providence in the sequel, that God made Absalom, whom his father's foolish pity spared, a scourge to him. The recall that looks like clemency is in truth a breach in the wall of justice.

The witnesses do not divide, and the verse stands as the pivot of the chapter's second half. All the art of the wise woman has issued in this: a convicted murderer, sheltered abroad, is brought home to the holy city by the commander of the army, without trial, without atonement, without repentance. The narrator reports it without comment, but the commentators supply the verdict the text implies — that a mercy which overrides law is no true mercy, and that the peace so purchased is a false peace. The homecoming of Absalom is the beginning of David's undoing, and the reader, like Henry, watches the great fly break through the web. What the wise woman won by her eloquence, and Joab by his scheming, was not the healing of a family but the loosing of a restraint that had held a dangerous man in check; and the swift, unremarkable clause that carries Absalom from Geshur to Jerusalem carries with it, though the text does not yet say so, the whole coming tragedy of the kingdom.
24:
David's half-measure — Let him turn to his own house, and let him not see my face — draws from the witnesses a rare unanimity of disapproval. The grammatical-historical commentators call it a blunder. Keil and Delitzsch judges that David could not forgive Absalom altogether, and that this half forgiveness was an imprudent measure which bore very bitter fruit. Ellicott is more precise still: the return had been allowed in weakness, the exclusion imposed through a sense of justice, but the combination was unfortunate, for Absalom was at once irritated and yet set in a position favourable to his plots; probably, Ellicott adds, he was confined to his own house.

The wesleyan and puritan voices weigh David's intention more kindly, while owning the mistake. Clarke sees the king declining to restore full favour at once as a proper way to show detestation of the crime and respect for justice, so that Absalom should not die for it and yet should feel its weight. Wesley reads the exclusion as designed both to avoid seeming to approve the sin and to draw Absalom toward a thorough humiliation and repentance. Matthew Henry agrees that the interdict served David's honour and his son's humbling, and thinks the king had heard something of Absalom's temper that gave him too much reason to doubt his penitence — so that, upon the first sign of true sorrow, David would gladly have received him wholly.

The witnesses converge on a single lesson: a partial reconciliation is often worse than none. To pardon the life yet withhold the presence, to bring the offender home yet hold him at arm's length, was to exasperate rather than to soften, and to give a discontented man both leisure and standing to conspire. Where full justice might have restrained Absalom and full grace might have won him, David's middle course did neither. The commentators see in it the same fatal softness that spared the murderer in the first place, and they mark it as the direct root of the rebellion that the following chapters will record. A king, they imply, must be wholly just or wholly gracious; the halfway house of a pardon without a presence pleased neither the demands of law nor the heart of the man it was meant to reclaim.
25:
The narrative pauses to praise Absalom's beauty — in all Israel there was none to be so much praised as Absalom for his beauty: from the sole of his foot even to the crown of his head there was no blemish in him — and the witnesses read the compliment as a warning in disguise. The grammatical-historical commentators note first its narrative purpose. Keil and Delitzsch takes the Hebrew idiom to much praising to mean that he was greatly praised, flawless from head to foot. Clarke sees the practical use of the note: it was Absalom's personal beauty that so caught the people and led them to interest themselves in his cause, for the great mass of men are ever taken by outward appearances.

The wesleyan Wesley states the point in a line: his beauty is recorded as the occasion both of his own pride and of the people's affection for him. The puritan Matthew Henry develops it into a meditation. Nothing is said here of Absalom's wisdom or piety — though the son of so devout a father, we read of no devotion in him — but only that he was handsome, which Henry calls a poor commendation for a man who had nothing else of worth. Many a polluted, deformed soul dwells in a fair and comely body, he writes, and Absalom's was polluted with a brother's blood and deformed with unnatural hatred of his father and king: in his body no blemish, in his mind nothing but wounds and bruises.

The witnesses do not divide, and the verse becomes a sustained irony. The historian's lavish praise of the prince's flawless form stands as a foil to the deep flaws of his character, and the commentators press the moral that comeliness is no measure of a man. Henry adds the sober application for parents: those who are better pleased with their children's beauty than with their virtue have reason to fear affliction in them, for it was perhaps Absalom's very comeliness that made David so fond and so slow to do him justice. The unblemished body will shortly be the instrument of a blemished ambition, and the beauty all Israel admired will help to carry the nation into revolt.
26:
The note on Absalom's hair — polled yearly because it grew too heavy, and weighing, when cut, two hundred shekels after the king's weight — sets the witnesses a famous puzzle of numbers and a lesson in vanity. The grammatical-historical commentators face the figure honestly. Keil and Delitzsch takes the strong growth of hair as a sign of manly vigour and so a proof of Absalom's beauty, but calls two hundred shekels a round number and far too high, whatever the difference between the royal and the sacred shekel. Ellicott agrees that the weight, on any reckoning, is very extraordinary, and suspects a clerical error in copying the number.

The wesleyan Clarke devotes a long dissertation to the problem, canvassing Bochart, Josephus, Epiphanius, and Hesychius. He rehearses how ancient numerals were single letters easily confused; how the two hundred shekels, if pressed, would make the hair weigh some pounds — Josephus even claiming it took eight days to cut; how much oil, ointment, and even gold-dust the ancients worked into their hair (Ps. 23:5; Eccl. 9:8); and how the king's shekel, by Epiphanius, was but the fourth of an ounce, the half-shekel of the sanctuary (Exod. 30:13) being the two drachms our Lord paid as tribute (Matt. 17:24). Clarke's own solution is that the letter for thirty was miscopied as the letter for two hundred, and that the hair was weighed at intervals, not yearly.

The puritan Matthew Henry leaves the arithmetic aside for the moral. This hair, he observes, is noted not as a Nazarite's — Absalom was far from that strictness — but as a fop's ornament: he let it grow till it was a burden, and would not cut it while he could bear the weight, for as pride feels no cold, so it feels no heat, and what feeds vanity is never felt as a trouble. When he did poll it, he had it weighed for ostentation, that men might see how it excelled. And Henry cannot forbear the grim sequel: this fine hair proved his halter, when it caught him in the oak and hung him between heaven and earth (2 Sam. 18:9). The witnesses thus turn a curiosity of measurement into a parable of pride, agreeing that the ornament Absalom cherished became the snare that destroyed him.
27:
The record of Absalom's children — three sons, and one daughter, whose name was Tamar: she was a woman of a fair countenance — is chiefly a matter for the grammatical-historical commentators, who read the silences and cross-references closely. Keil and Delitzsch observes that, against custom, the sons are not named, most likely because they died in infancy; and this is confirmed by Absalom's own later complaint that he had no son to keep his name in remembrance, which drove him to erect a memorial pillar (2 Sam. 18:18). Gill and Wesley reach the same conclusion — the sons died young — and Gill notes that this explains the monument Absalom raised for want of an heir.

The daughter draws a warmer notice. Keil takes her name, Tamar, and her beauty as a proof of Absalom's tender regard for the sister whom Amnon had violated — a quiet act of family loyalty preserved in the record. Around her the commentators debate a textual addition. Clarke and Ellicott both note that the Septuagint adds that Tamar became the wife of Rehoboam, Solomon's son, and bore him Abia. Ellicott judges this a confused gloss: since the wife of Rehoboam and mother of Abijah was Maachah, the daughter of Absalom (1 Kings 15:2), Maachah must in fact have been Absalom's granddaughter through Tamar, who married Uriel — so that Absalom's line, failing in his sons, ran on through his daughter into the royal house of Judah.

The witnesses do not seriously divide, and the verse yields a poignant footnote to Absalom's story. The beautiful prince whose sons all died, and who set up a lifeless pillar to bear the name he could not pass on by blood, is nonetheless remembered through a daughter named for a wronged and beloved aunt. The historian's spare genealogy, weighed by the commentators against the later chapters and the books of Kings, thus opens a small window on the mingled affection and futility of Absalom's house — a man consumed by ambition, bereft of heirs, yet tender enough to name his one daughter after his injured sister, and survived, in the end, only through her.
28:
So Absalom dwelt two full years in Jerusalem, and saw not the king's face. The witnesses read the long stalemate and Absalom's violent way of breaking it. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch narrates the plan: after two whole years of exclusion Absalom sent for Joab, hoping through him to obtain the king's full forgiveness; and when Joab, twice summoned, would not come, Absalom ordered his servants to set fire to Joab's barley field, which adjoined his own, foreseeing that the general would not take the loss quietly but would come to complain. Gill supplies the same sequence of an impatient prince chafing under confinement.

The puritan Matthew Henry probes the state of Absalom's soul through these five years — three in exile, two a prisoner at large — and finds him, though better dealt with than he deserved, still unhumbled and unmortified. Had he truly repented, his distance from the gaieties of the court and his retirement in the holy city would have suited him well; if a murderer must live, yet let him be for ever a recluse. But Absalom could not bear so gentle a mortification. He longed to see the king's face, pretending love, but really wanting the opportunity to supplant his father; this snake cannot sting again till he be warmed in his father's bosom. And he gained his point, Henry marvels, not by submission or promises of reformation but by insults and injuries.

The witnesses agree that the verse exposes the character behind the beauty. Two years of enforced quietness, which a penitent might have received as mercy, only sharpened Absalom's resentment, and the first use he made of his liberty was an act of arson against the man who had procured his recall. The contrast the commentators draw is stark: David's half-grace was meant to lead his son toward repentance, but it found in Absalom a heart that read forbearance as weakness and mercy as delay. The stage is set for the outrage of the next verses, where the prince will compel by fire the reconciliation he would not seek by sorrow. Henry's aphorism hangs over the whole scene: a mercy misapplied to the impenitent hardens the heart it was meant to heal.
29:
Therefore Absalom sent for Joab, to have sent him to the king; but he would not come to him: and when he sent again the second time, he would not come. The witnesses attend to Joab's refusal and its probable reasons. Gill notes the prince's motive — uneasy at being confined to his own house and shut from the court, he wanted Joab to introduce him to the king. Keil and Delitzsch simply records the twice-repeated summons and the twice-repeated refusal that will provoke Absalom's drastic remedy.

The grammatical-historical Ellicott supplies the likeliest grounds for Joab's coldness. The general felt he had already gone far enough in procuring Absalom's return, and, since the prince still lay under the king's displeasure, he was not disposed to do anything more; and possibly, Ellicott adds, he thought Absalom should first have shown some sign of penitence for his great crime. The very man who had schemed so elaborately to bring Absalom home now keeps his distance, unwilling to spend more credit on a cause that had grown dangerous and on a client who showed no contrition.

The puritan Matthew Henry reads Joab's reluctance as the natural recoil from Absalom's pride. Proud men, he observes, take every service done them as a debt, and Absalom had not owned Joab's earlier kindness in bringing him to Jerusalem as gratefully as he ought; one in his position should rather have sent a courteous message and a generous gift, as courtiers expect, than have presumed upon the general's help. Instead of courting the man whose favour he needed, the prince presumed upon a debt he imagined Joab owed him, and so met refusal with resentment.

The witnesses do not divide: they see a stand-off between two hard men, the arrogant prince who will not stoop and the wary commander who will not come, and they watch the impasse drive Absalom to the lawless expedient that the next verse records. The irony is that Joab, who had gone to such lengths to fetch Absalom home, now recoils from him the moment he perceives no penitence; the schemer's zeal cools exactly where the client's pride refuses to bend. Each man is waiting for the other to move, and the deadlock between them, trivial in itself, becomes the occasion for an act of arson that will drag the whole matter, at last, before the king. What patience and courtesy could not accomplish, the commentators note, Absalom will shortly extort by damage and threat.
30:
Absalom's remedy — See, Joab's field is near mine, and he hath barley there; go and set it on fire — is read by the witnesses as a stroke at once effective and lawless. Gill notes plainly that he ordered his own servants to fire the general's crop. The wesleyan Clarke owns that the conduct was strange, but that it had the desired effect: Joab, who had not stirred to bring Absalom to court, now bestirs himself, and succeeds. The grammatical-historical Ellicott is blunter — the stratagem was perfectly successful, but such a thing would only have been resorted to by a lawless and unscrupulous character.

The puritan Matthew Henry presses both the malice and the strangeness of the act. It was as spiteful a thing as Absalom could do; Samson himself could think of no greater injury to the Philistines than to burn their standing corn (Judg. 15:5). Strange, Henry marvels, that Absalom should hope, by doing Joab a mischief, to win a kindness from him, or that a man should recommend himself to prince and people by showing himself so malicious and so careless of the public good, for the fire might well spread to the corn of others. Yet by this means he brought Joab to him, and Henry draws the analogy that God, by afflictions, brings to himself those who kept at a distance. He notes too that Absalom was bound by the law to make restitution (Exod. 22:6), though we do not read that he offered it or that Joab demanded it.

The witnesses do not divide, and the verse completes the portrait of Absalom's character before his reconciliation. The same man whose beauty charmed the nation is here revealed as one who will burn a neighbour's harvest to bend him to his will — a preview, the commentators feel, of the ruthlessness that will soon turn against the king himself. That such an outrage should be rewarded with the very interview he sought only deepens the chapter's warning: David's court has learned to yield to boldness and threats, and the prince has learned that fire and effrontery accomplish what patience and penitence never would.
31:
The single sentence of this verse — Then Joab arose, and came to Absalom unto his house, and said unto him, Wherefore have thy servants set my field on fire? — draws only a brief remark from the reformed Gill, who observes that Joab came provoked at the outrage and demanding its reason, and that this was precisely the end Absalom had aimed at. Yet the brevity of the witnesses' comment should not hide the completeness of Absalom's triumph, which the verse quietly records.

The point on which everything turns is that the arson worked. The general who had twice refused to come at a prince's polite summons now rises and goes to that prince's house of his own accord, brought not by courtesy but by damage. Absalom has correctly judged his man: Joab would ignore a request but not an injury, would slight an invitation but not a burnt field. The question Joab arrives asking — wherefore have thy servants set my field on fire? — is itself the proof that Absalom has succeeded, for it means the general is standing, angry but present, exactly where the prince wanted him.

There is no debate among the witnesses, and the verse serves as the narrow hinge between the outrage and its reward. Commentators on the surrounding verses note the grim comedy of it: Absalom does Joab a wrong in order to obtain a favour, and the method that ought to have earned him further disgrace instead procures the interview and, through it, the restoration he craved. The lesson the expositors draw from the passage as a whole is here compressed into a single exchange — that in David's kingdom, at this low ebb, the way to be heard is not to entreat but to threaten, and that a man daring enough to burn what he cannot beg for will get his audience. Joab's arrival is Absalom's victory, and the way now lies open to the insolent message of the verse that follows. The witnesses let the plain sentence speak: the prince who could not purchase an interview with civility has bought it with fire, and the transaction tells us all we need to know of the man David is about to receive back into his favour.
32:
Absalom's answer to Joab is unrepentant to the last: Wherefore am I come from Geshur? it had been good for me to have been there still: now therefore let me see the king's face; and if there be any iniquity in me, let him kill me. The witnesses read it as a demand rather than a plea. Gill notes that he neither denies the deed, nor is ashamed of it, nor asks pardon, but sets out to vindicate himself. The grammatical-historical Ellicott puts it exactly: Absalom makes no acknowledgment of having done wrong, but simply declares that this state of half-reconciliation is intolerable — he must either be punished or fully pardoned; and Joab's intercession accomplishes its purpose, so that the king receives him. In this, Ellicott adds, David showed great weakness, for which he afterwards suffered severely.

The puritan Matthew Henry hears in the message an insolence unbecoming a son or a subject. Absalom undervalues the favour shown him in his recall from banishment and his restoration to his own house in Jerusalem — Wherefore have I come from Geshur? — denies his own notorious crime, and defies the king's justice with let him kill me, knowing well that David loved him too much to do it. The wesleyan Wesley reads the same defiance as a calculated pathos: kill me means that it were better to die than to want the sight and favour of his dear father, a stroke by which he insinuates himself into David's affections; having so far recovered favour, Absalom now grows bold enough to stand upon his own justification, as if his deed had been no iniquity deserving death.

The witnesses converge on a somber verdict. There is not a word of contrition in Absalom's speech, only the confidence of a man who has taken his father's measure and found him weak; and Wesley draws the general lesson, that we may see how easily wise parents are imposed upon by children whom they blindly love. Absalom demands as a right what he ought to have begged as a mercy, and turns the very generosity of the king into the lever of his ambition. The message that should have earned rebuke will, in the next verse, earn a kiss — and the commentators let that dreadful ease stand as the measure of David's folly and the prelude to his grief.
33:
The chapter closes on the reconciliation: So Joab came to the king, and told him: and when he had called for Absalom, he came to the king, and bowed himself on his face to the ground before the king: and the king kissed Absalom. The witnesses read the kiss as both a tender and a tragic act. Gill and Keil and Delitzsch record the sequence plainly — Joab reports, the king summons, the prince prostrates himself, and David seals the pardon. The grammatical-historical Ellicott, weighing the whole episode, judges that in receiving and kissing Absalom in token of complete reconciliation David showed great weakness, for which he afterwards suffered severely.

The puritan Matthew Henry exposes the mutual self-deception of the moment. David's strong affection construed Absalom's insolent posture as the language of great respect and an earnest desire of his favour, when it was far otherwise; and Henry warns how easily wise and good men may be imposed upon by their own children who design ill, especially when they are blindly fond of them. Absalom bowed to the ground, testifying a submission he did not feel, and David with a kiss sealed a pardon the son had never sought in penitence.

Yet the witnesses will not let the scene end in mere folly, for they find in it, against the grain, a gleam of the gospel. Both Henry and Wesley turn the father's kiss into a question to the sinner: did the bowels of a father prevail to reconcile David to an impenitent son, and shall penitent sinners question the compassion of him who is the Father of mercies? If Ephraim do but bemoan himself, God soon bemoans him with all the expressions of a fatherly tenderness, owning him still a dear son, a pleasant child (Jer. 31:20). The commentators hold the two truths together without flinching: David's reconciliation was a lapse of justice that would breed rebellion and sorrow, and yet the very weakness of his love, misplaced upon an unworthy son, becomes a faint and broken image of the far greater mercy of God, who kisses the returning penitent and asks no price but his coming home.
